schools.summary: The glorious herald: 'and after these things I saw another angel come down from heaven, having great power; and the earth was lightened with his glory' — a mighty angel descending from heaven with great power, the earth illumined by his glory (the splendor of the angel marking the importance of the announcement, Babylon's fall, he brings, his glory lighting the whole earth); 'having great power (authority)' a high, mighty angel fitted to proclaim the doom of the great city (the dignity of the messenger befitting the greatness of the message); 'the earth was lightened with his glory' the angel's brightness filling the earth (whether marking the diffusion of the gospel-light at Babylon's fall, or simply the surpassing glory of the herald, the radiance announcing the great proclamation) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: another angel come down from heaven, having great power; and the earth was lightened with his glory
the glorious angel announcing Babylon's fall
the majestic herald of the great city's doom
the radiance filling the earth

schools.summary: The proclamation of Babylon's fall: 'and he cried mightily with a strong voice, saying, Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen, and is become the habitation of devils, and the hold of every foul spirit, and a cage of every unclean and hateful bird' — the angel proclaiming, with a mighty voice, the fall of Babylon (the repetition 'is fallen, is fallen,' echoing 14:8; Isaiah 21:9, marking the certainty and completeness of her ruin); 'is become the habitation of devils (demons), and the hold (haunt) of every foul spirit' (her ruins the dwelling of demons and unclean spirits, the figure of utter desolation and abandonment to evil, drawn from Isaiah 13:21-22; 34:11-15, the desolation of old Babylon and Edom, the once-great city becoming a haunt of demons); 'a cage (haunt) of every unclean and hateful bird' (the desolate ruins inhabited only by unclean birds, the picture of total, loathsome desolation, the proud city reduced to a wilderness of unclean creatures) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen
is become the habitation of devils, and the hold of every foul spirit, and a cage of every unclean and hateful bird
the proclamation of Babylon's fall and desolation
the great city reduced to loathsome desolation

schools.summary: The threefold cause of her fall: 'for all nations have drunk of the wine of the wrath of her fornication, and the kings of the earth have committed fornication with her, and the merchants of the earth are waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies' — the nations having drunk the intoxicating wine of her fornication (14:8; 17:2, she seducing and corrupting all peoples with her idolatry and false worship, her universal corruption a ground of her judgment); the kings entering into corrupt alliance with her (17:2, sharing her idolatry and worldly corruption, their fornication with her a further ground of judgment); the merchants growing rich by her excessive luxury and self-indulgence ('the abundance of her delicacies' — luxury, wantonness, her wealth and sensuality enriching the traders, the third ground her proud luxurious wantonness, the merchants' lament, vv.11-19, following from their dependence on her trade) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: all nations have drunk of the wine of the wrath of her fornication
the kings of the earth have committed fornication with her, and the merchants of the earth are waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies
the threefold cause of Babylon's fall
her corruption of the world and her proud sensuality

schools.summary: The call to come out: 'and I heard another voice from heaven, saying, Come out of her, my people, that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her plagues' — a voice from heaven calling God's people to come out of Babylon (echoing Jeremiah 51:6, 45; Isaiah 52:11, the summons to separate from the doomed, corrupt city, lest they share her sins and her judgment); 'that ye be not partakers of her sins' (by remaining in Babylon, God's people would lend their sanction to her sins and be implicated in them, the call to separate a call to purity, to come out from her corruption, cf. 2 Corinthians 6:17, in the historicist reading the duty to separate wholly from corrupt papal Rome); 'that ye receive not of her plagues' (those who remain would share her plagues, v.8, the call also for safety, to escape her doom, God calling his own out before the judgment falls that they perish not with her) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Come out of her, my people, that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her plagues
the call to come out of Babylon
the call to separate from the doomed, corrupt city
for purity and for safety from her judgment

schools.summary: The accumulated guilt: 'for her sins have reached unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities' — Babylon's sins having piled up to heaven (clung together, reached up, like the tower of Babel, Genesis 11; cf. Jeremiah 51:9), and God having remembered her iniquities (the accumulated, heaven-high guilt now coming into remembrance for judgment); 'her sins have reached unto heaven' (her iniquities so many and so great that they reach to heaven, the figure of an immense, accumulated mass of guilt crying out for judgment); 'God hath remembered her iniquities' (God, who seemed to delay, now remembering her sins, 16:19, his remembrance issuing in judgment, the time of reckoning for her long-accumulated iniquity come, God not forgetting the sins of the persecutor) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: her sins have reached unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities
Babylon's sins reaching heaven and remembered by God
the accumulated, heaven-high guilt brought to reckoning
God's remembrance issuing in judgment

schools.summary: The call for double retribution: 'reward her even as she rewarded you, and double unto her double according to her works: in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double' — Babylon to be repaid as she dealt with others, and doubly, according to her works (the principle of just, indeed redoubled, retribution, she who filled the cup of corruption to be made to drink a double measure of judgment); 'reward her even as she rewarded you' calling for retribution in kind (she repaid for her treatment of God's people, the persecutor requited, the call addressed to the agents of judgment, or the saints' vindication); 'double unto her double... in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double' the doubled measure (Jeremiah 16:18; 50:29, a full and overflowing measure, the cup of judgment filled double the cup of corruption she filled, the figure of complete, redoubled recompense for her sins) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Reward her even as she rewarded you, and double unto her double according to her works
in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double
the call for double retribution on Babylon
the just, redoubled recompense on the corrupt city

schools.summary: Babylon's pride answered: 'how much she hath glorified herself, and lived deliciously, so much torment and sorrow give her: for she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow' — the measure of her self-glorification and luxury the measure of her coming torment and sorrow (her pride and self-indulgence, living deliciously/luxuriously/wantonly, repaid with proportionate suffering, the higher she exalted herself the deeper her fall); 'she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow' her proud presumption (Babylon presuming she reigns securely as a queen, is no bereaved widow, and will never see sorrow, echoing Isaiah 47:7-8, the boast of old Babylon, her proud false security inviting her sudden ruin); 'shall see no sorrow' the false security (she presuming she is beyond calamity, secure in her power and wealth, this very presumption answered by the sudden, total judgment, v.8) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: How much she hath glorified herself, and lived deliciously, so much torment and sorrow give her
she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow
Babylon's pride answered by torment
her proud false security repaid with sudden ruin

schools.summary: Babylon's sudden destruction: 'therefore shall her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine; and she shall be utterly burned with fire: for strong is the Lord God who judgeth her' — her plagues falling in a single day (suddenly, all at once, death, mourning, and famine coming swiftly, the abruptness of her ruin contrasting her presumption of lasting security, v.7); 'she shall be utterly burned with fire' (the corrupt city consumed by fire, 17:16; 18:9, 18, the complete, fiery destruction of Babylon, the burning marking her total and irreversible ruin); 'strong (mighty) is the Lord God who judgeth her' (the reason her ruin is sure and total: the God who judges her is mighty, against his strength she cannot stand, her power nothing before the Almighty, the certainty of the judgment resting on God's might) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine; and she shall be utterly burned with fire
for strong is the Lord God who judgeth her
Babylon's sudden and total destruction
the swift, complete ruin of the proud city before the Almighty

schools.summary: The kings' lament: 'and the kings of the earth, who have committed fornication and lived deliciously with her, shall bewail her, and lament for her, when they shall see the smoke of her burning' — the kings who shared her fornication and luxury (living deliciously/luxuriously with her) now bewailing and lamenting her fall (the first of three laments — kings, merchants, mariners — the partners in her sin mourning her ruin, not in repentance but in self-interest); 'when they shall see the smoke of her burning' (the rising smoke of the burning city, v.8; cf. Genesis 19:28, the sight that draws their lament, the visible token of her destruction); the kings mourning her not for her sin but for the loss of the luxury and gain they shared with her (their grief for themselves, for the pleasures and profits now gone, the lament of those who loved her corruption) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: the kings of the earth, who have committed fornication and lived deliciously with her, shall bewail her, and lament for her
when they shall see the smoke of her burning
the kings lamenting Babylon's burning
the partners in her sin mourning her ruin out of self-interest

schools.summary: The kings' cry of woe: 'standing afar off for the fear of her torment, saying, Alas, alas, that great city Babylon, that mighty city! for in one hour is thy judgment come' — the kings standing at a distance, afraid of sharing her torment, and crying their lament (they dare not come near but mourn from afar, their fear keeping them back even as they grieve); 'Alas, alas (woe, woe), that great city Babylon, that mighty city' the dirge over the fallen city (the doubled 'alas' the lament over her former greatness and might now brought to ruin, the contrast of her former glory and present destruction); 'in one hour is thy judgment come' (the kings marveling at the swiftness of her fall, in a single hour her judgment come, the abrupt, sudden ruin of the mighty city, the refrain of suddenness, vv.10, 17, 19, astonishing the onlookers) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Standing afar off for the fear of her torment, saying, Alas, alas, that great city Babylon, that mighty city
for in one hour is thy judgment come
the kings' cry of woe over the fallen city
the marvel at the sudden ruin of the mighty city

schools.summary: The merchants' lament: 'and the merchants of the earth shall weep and mourn over her; for no man buyeth their merchandise any more' — the merchants weeping and mourning over Babylon's fall (the second of the three laments, their grief for the loss of their trade — no one buys their goods any more now that the great market-city is destroyed); 'the merchants of the earth shall weep and mourn over her' (the traders who grew rich by her, v.3, lamenting her ruin, like the kings their grief self-interested, for their own loss); 'for no man buyeth their merchandise any more' (the cause of their grief plainly the collapse of their trade, with Babylon, their great customer and market, destroyed, their merchandise unsold, their mourning for lost profit not for her sin, the self-interest of the merchants laid bare) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: the merchants of the earth shall weep and mourn over her; for no man buyeth their merchandise any more
the merchants mourning the loss of their trade
the second lament, self-interested for lost profit
the collapse of the great market-city

schools.summary: The catalogue of luxury wares: 'the merchandise of gold, and silver, and precious stones, and of pearls, and fine linen, and purple, and silk, and scarlet, and all thyine wood, and all manner vessels of ivory, and all manner vessels of most precious wood, and of brass, and iron, and marble' — a long catalogue of the costly, luxurious wares traded by Babylon (gold, silver, jewels, pearls, fine fabrics — linen, purple, silk, scarlet — precious woods, ivory, and costly metals and stone, the wealth and luxury of the great market-city); the list (precious metals, gems, fine cloth, rare woods, ivory, marble) marking the immense luxury and wealth of Babylon (the merchandise of a city given to extravagance and display, her commerce in all things rare and costly); the detailed inventory (continued in v.13) displaying the vast, varied luxury of Babylon and the extent of the merchants' loss (the listing of her wares emphasizing the worldly opulence now brought to nothing, the abundance of her delicacies, v.3, catalogued) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: the merchandise of gold, and silver, and precious stones, and of pearls, and fine linen, and purple, and silk, and scarlet
all manner vessels of ivory... and of brass, and iron, and marble
the catalogue of Babylon's luxury merchandise
the vast, varied opulence now brought to nothing

schools.summary: The catalogue continued: 'and cinnamon, and odours, and ointments, and frankincense, and wine, and oil, and fine flour, and wheat, and beasts, and sheep, and horses, and chariots, and slaves, and souls of men' — the catalogue continuing with spices, perfumes, incense, wine, oil, food, livestock, and chariots (the wares of luxury, religion, food, and transport, the full range of Babylon's commerce); the inclusion of animals, horses, and chariots (the staples and luxuries of trade, the commerce extending from precious goods to beasts and vehicles); the catalogue ending climactically with 'slaves (somaton — bodies), and souls of men' (Babylon trafficking even in human beings, in slaves and the souls/lives of men, the climactic, damning item — the trade in human persons, the inhumanity of her commerce that bought and sold men's bodies and souls, the gravest mark of her corruption) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: cinnamon, and odours, and ointments, and frankincense, and wine, and oil, and fine flour, and wheat, and beasts, and sheep, and horses, and chariots
and slaves, and souls of men
the catalogue continued, ending with slaves and souls of men
the inhuman traffic marking the depth of her corruption

schools.summary: The vanished luxuries: 'and the fruits that thy soul lusted after are departed from thee, and all things which were dainty and goodly are departed from thee, and thou shalt find them no more at all' — the ripe fruits (the autumn fruits, the choice luxuries) her soul craved gone, all her dainty and splendid things departed and never to be found again (the total, irreversible loss of her luxuries); 'the fruits that thy soul lusted after' the luxuries and pleasures she craved and gloried in, now departed (the objects of her sensual desire taken away); 'all things which were dainty and goodly are departed... thou shalt find them no more at all' (the loss total and permanent, her splendid luxurious things gone forever, the emphatic finality 'no more at all' marking the irreversible ruin of her luxury) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the fruits that thy soul lusted after are departed from thee, and all things which were dainty and goodly are departed from thee
thou shalt find them no more at all
the vanished luxuries of Babylon
the total, irreversible loss of her luxury

schools.summary: The merchants' distant lament: 'the merchants of these things, which were made rich by her, shall stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing' — the merchants who grew rich by trading with Babylon standing at a distance, afraid of her torment, weeping and wailing (like the kings, v.10, they keep their distance even as they mourn, their grief for their lost wealth); 'which were made rich by her' (their wealth came from her trade, v.3, their lament for the loss of the riches she brought them, their mourning self-interested); 'stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing' (afraid to come near lest they share her judgment, they mourn from a distance, their weeping and wailing for themselves, for the loss of their gain, the self-interested lament of the enriched traders) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: The merchants of these things, which were made rich by her, shall stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing
the merchants standing afar in fear and grief
the enriched merchants mourning their lost wealth from afar
the self-interested lament of the traders

schools.summary: The merchants' dirge: 'and saying, Alas, alas, that great city, that was clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet, and decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls!' — the merchants, like the kings (v.10), crying 'Alas, alas' over the great city (they lament her former splendor — clothed in fine linen, purple, and scarlet, decked with gold, jewels, and pearls, echoing the harlot's array, 17:4, the dirge over her vanished magnificence); 'clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet' her former luxurious attire (the very wares they sold her, her splendor, drawn from 17:4, recalled in her ruin); 'decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls' (the merchants recalling her former opulence, the gold and jewels of their trade, now lost, the contrast of her past magnificence and present ruin sharpening their lament, they mourning the splendor that enriched them) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: Alas, alas, that great city, that was clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet
decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls
the merchants' dirge over the splendid city
mourning the vanished magnificence that enriched them

schools.summary: The sudden ruin and the mariners' lament: 'for in one hour so great riches is come to nought. And every shipmaster, and all the company in ships, and sailors, and as many as trade by sea, stood afar off' — the merchants marveling that in a single hour such great wealth has come to nothing (the suddenness of the ruin, the refrain, vv.10, 19, astonishing them, the vast riches of Babylon destroyed in an hour); 'every shipmaster, and all the company in ships, and sailors, and as many as trade by sea' the third group of mourners (the mariners and seafarers who carried her commerce joining the lament, the third of the three laments — kings, merchants, mariners — all who profited by her sea-trade mourning her fall); 'stood afar off' (like the kings, v.10, and merchants, v.15, they keep their distance, mourning from afar, the seafarers lamenting the loss of the trade that enriched them, the third group of self-interested mourners standing at a distance) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: in one hour so great riches is come to nought
every shipmaster, and all the company in ships, and sailors, and as many as trade by sea, stood afar off
the sudden loss of riches and the mariners' lament
the third group of self-interested mourners

schools.summary: The mariners' cry: 'and cried when they saw the smoke of her burning, saying, What city is like unto this great city!' — the mariners, seeing the smoke of her burning (v.9), crying out, 'What city is like unto this great city!' (they lament her as unequaled, marveling that so great a city is destroyed, the dirge over her incomparable greatness now in ruins); 'when they saw the smoke of her burning' (the rising smoke, as v.9, the sight that draws their lament, the visible token of her destruction prompting their cry); 'What city is like unto this great city' (the mariners lamenting her as without equal, the cry, echoing Ezekiel 27:32, the lament over Tyre, marking her as the unrivaled great city now fallen, the wonder that so incomparable a city should perish) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: cried when they saw the smoke of her burning, saying, What city is like unto this great city!
the mariners' cry at the smoke of her burning
the dirge over her incomparable greatness now in ruins
the lament over the unrivaled city's fall

schools.summary: The mariners' mourning: 'and they cast dust on their heads, and cried, weeping and wailing, saying, Alas, alas, that great city, wherein were made rich all that had ships in the sea by reason of her costliness! for in one hour is she made desolate' — the mariners casting dust on their heads (the gesture of deep grief and mourning, Ezekiel 27:30; Lamentations 2:10) and crying 'Alas, alas' over the great city (the third lament reaching its climax of grief); 'wherein were made rich all that had ships in the sea by reason of her costliness (her wealth)' (the seafarers grew rich by her costly trade, their lament for their own enrichment now ended, the mariners mourning the source of their wealth); 'in one hour is she made desolate' (the refrain, vv.10, 17, of the sudden, swift desolation, the great rich city made desolate in a single hour, the abruptness of her ruin marking the closing of the threefold lament) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they cast dust on their heads, and cried, weeping and wailing, saying, Alas, alas, that great city
for in one hour is she made desolate
the mariners' mourning and the desolation of the city
the climax of the threefold lament over the suddenly desolate city

schools.summary: The call to rejoice: 'rejoice over her, thou heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets; for God hath avenged you on her' — in pointed contrast to the lament of the kings, merchants, and mariners, heaven and the holy apostles and prophets called to rejoice over Babylon's fall (the saints and heaven rejoicing where the worldly mourn); 'heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets (and saints)' the rejoicers (the redeemed and the heavenly host, especially the apostles and prophets whom Babylon persecuted, those who suffered from her now rejoicing in her judgment); 'for God hath avenged you (judged your judgment) on her' the vindication (God has executed the saints' cause against her, avenging the blood of the persecuted, cf. 6:10; 18:24, the rejoicing grounded in God's vindication of his people, her fall the answer to the martyrs' cry) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: Rejoice over her, thou heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets; for God hath avenged you on her
the call for heaven and the saints to rejoice over Babylon's fall
the heavenly counterpart to the earthly lament
the saints rejoicing in God's vindication

schools.summary: The millstone cast into the sea: 'and a mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone, and cast it into the sea, saying, Thus with violence shall that great city Babylon be thrown down, and shall be found no more at all' — a mighty angel performing a symbolic act (as Jeremiah 51:63-64, where the scroll with a stone is cast into the Euphrates), casting a great millstone into the sea (the violent, irreversible sinking of the stone signifying the violent, total overthrow of Babylon); 'Thus with violence shall that great city Babylon be thrown down' (as the millstone is hurled down and sinks, so Babylon will be violently, suddenly cast down, the figure of her sudden, forceful, irreversible destruction); 'and shall be found no more at all' (like the stone sunk in the sea, Babylon gone forever, never to be found again, the emphatic finality marking her total, permanent ruin, she vanishing utterly) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: a mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone, and cast it into the sea
Thus with violence shall that great city Babylon be thrown down, and shall be found no more at all
the millstone cast into the sea, the sign of Babylon's fall
her sudden, total, irreversible ruin

schools.summary: The silence of the desolate city: 'and the voice of harpers, and musicians, and of pipers, and trumpeters, shall be heard no more at all in thee; and no craftsman, of whatsoever craft he be, shall be found any more in thee; and the sound of a millstone shall be heard no more at all in thee' — the music (harpers, musicians, pipers, trumpeters), the work of craftsmen, and the sound of the millstone (the everyday labor of grinding) all ceasing in Babylon (the figure of a city utterly desolate, where all the sounds of festive and productive life are silenced, drawn from Jeremiah 25:10; Ezekiel 26:13); the cessation of music marking the end of all festivity and joy (no more songs or celebrations, the silence of a dead city); the absence of craftsmen and the silence of the millstone marking the end of all industry and daily life (no more work, no more bread ground, the city wholly desolate, devoid of both joy and labor) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: the voice of harpers, and musicians, and of pipers, and trumpeters, shall be heard no more at all in thee
no craftsman... and the sound of a millstone shall be heard no more at all in thee
the silence of music and labor in the desolate city
the city wholly desolate, devoid of joy and labor

schools.summary: The darkness and the cause: 'and the light of a candle shall shine no more at all in thee; and the voice of the bridegroom and of the bride shall be heard no more at all in thee: for thy merchants were the great men of the earth; for by thy sorceries were all nations deceived' — no lamp shall shine, and no voice of bridegroom and bride (the sounds of joy, marriage, and home, Jeremiah 25:10) shall be heard (the city dark and joyless, all light and gladness extinguished, total desolation); 'thy merchants were the great men (megistanes — magnates, princes) of the earth' (her traders the world's great men, her commerce making her merchants princely, her former greatness and worldly power recalled in her ruin); 'by thy sorceries (pharmakeia — enchantments, magic arts) were all nations deceived' (Babylon deceived all nations by her sorceries, whether literal magic or the figure of her seductive, corrupting deceptions — her false worship, allurements, and intrigues — the cause of her judgment her deception and corruption of the nations) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the light of a candle shall shine no more at all in thee; and the voice of the bridegroom and of the bride shall be heard no more
for by thy sorceries were all nations deceived
the darkness and silence of the city and the cause of her fall
the dark, silent city judged for her deceptions

schools.summary: The blood of the saints found in her: 'and in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints, and of all that were slain upon the earth' — Babylon found guilty of the blood of the prophets and saints (she the great persecutor, in whom the blood of God's slain people is found, the chief ground of her judgment her bloodguilt against the faithful); 'the blood of prophets, and of saints' (the martyred prophets and faithful found in her, she killed God's people, 17:6; 18:20, her persecution of the saints bringing her judgment); 'and of all that were slain upon the earth' the comprehensive guilt (the blood of all the martyrs, indeed of all unjustly slain, charged to her, as Jerusalem in Matthew 23:35, the great persecuting power held responsible for the blood of God's people through the ages, her comprehensive bloodguilt sealing her doom) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints, and of all that were slain upon the earth
the blood of the saints found in Babylon
the great persecutor guilty of the martyrs' blood
her comprehensive bloodguilt sealing her doom
